preamble.summary: A psalm that indicts corrupt rulers who fail to administer justice, compares them to venomous snakes deaf to the charmer, and calls on God to shatter their power through a series of vivid destruction-images. The psalm concludes with the righteous celebrating when divine justice is vindicated.
preamble.remarkable: This psalm is among the most violent in the Psalter, and that violence is not random — it is a cry against systemic injustice from those who have no access to earthly courts of appeal. The six destruction-images in verses 7-10 (broken teeth, vanishing water, blunted arrows, dissolving snails, stillborn children, thorns swept by storm) are not sadistic fantasy but the accumulated fury of people whose only recourse is God. The deaf-adder image (vv. 5-6) is psychologically precise: the corrupt judge is not ignorant of justice — he has stopped his ears against it, like a snake that deliberately refuses to hear the charmer. The corruption is willful, not accidental.
preamble.friction: The opening word elem (v. 2) is notoriously difficult. It could mean 'silence' (are you truly silent when justice is needed?), 'gods' (elim, addressing divine beings of Psalm 82), or 'rulers' (a metonymic use). The WLC reads elem, which most naturally yields 'silence' or 'in silence.' We follow the reading that addresses human rulers who maintain guilty silence when justice demands speech. Verse 11 (Hebrew), where the righteous 'wash their feet in the blood of the wicked,' has troubled readers throughout history. It is imprecatory language that imagines total vindication — the image is victory, not cruelty.
preamble.connections: The corrupt-judge theme connects to Psalm 82, where God judges the 'gods' (rulers) who pervert justice. The deaf-snake imagery has parallels in Egyptian and Mesopotamian wisdom literature about snake-charming. The call for God to break teeth (v. 7) echoes Psalm 3:7. The righteous-rejoicing conclusion connects to the Song of Moses (Exodus 15) and the celebration after Haman's fall (Esther 8). Paul's use of 'snake venom under their lips' in Romans 3:13 draws from this psalm tradition.

verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician, Altaschith, Michtam of David.
verses.rendering:
For the director of music. "Do Not Destroy."
A miktam of David.
translator_notes: The al tashchet ('do not destroy') designation continues from Psalm 57, linking these psalms in a thematic cluster. The miktam label connects all six psalms in this group (56-60).

verses.text_hebrew: הַֽאֻמְנָ֗ם אֵ֣לֶם צֶ֭דֶק תְּדַבֵּר֑וּן מֵישָׁרִ֥ים תִּ֝שְׁפְּט֗וּ בְּנֵ֣י אָדָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Do ye indeed speak righteousness, O congregation? do ye judge uprightly, O ye sons of men?
verses.rendering:
Do you rulers truly speak what is right?
Do you judge the children of humanity with equity?
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm opens with a challenge hurled at those in power. The word elem is deeply contested — it may mean 'silence' (accusation: you are silent when justice demands speech), 'gods/rulers' (addressing the powerful as quasi-divine figures, as in Psalm 82), or come from a root meaning 'binding, obligation.' The rendering 'rulers' captures the functional meaning: these are people with the authority and obligation to speak tsedeq ('righteousness, justice') and to judge meisharim ('with equity, with uprightness'). The rhetorical questions expect negative answers: no, you do not speak what is right. No, you do not judge fairly. The questions are indictments.
translator_notes: The pairing of tsedeq ('righteousness') and meisharim ('equity, uprightness') describes the two essential qualities of a just ruler: declaring what is right (tsedeq) and applying it fairly to all (meisharim). These rulers have failed at both.

verses.text_hebrew: אַף־בְּ֭לֵב עוֹלֹ֣ת תִּפְעָל֑וּן בָּ֝אָ֗רֶץ חֲמַ֤ס יְדֵיכֶ֬ם תְּפַלֵּסֽוּן׃
verses.text_kjv: Yea, in heart ye work wickedness; ye weigh the violence of your hands in the earth.
verses.rendering:
No — in your hearts you devise injustice;
on earth your hands weigh out violence.
verses.expanded_rendering: The answer to the rhetorical question is not silence but af ('indeed, even, moreover') — an emphatic particle that introduces the accusation. be-lev ('in the heart') — the corruption begins internally, in the seat of will and decision. What they produce: olot ('injustice, wickedness'). And on the ground: chamas yedeikhem tefallesun ('the violence of your hands you weigh out'). The verb pilles means 'to level, to weigh, to make level' — it is the verb for the scales of justice. These rulers use the instruments of justice (scales, measures) to distribute chamas ('violence'). The image is devastating: the scales that should measure fairness are used to measure out harm.
translator_notes: The inversion of justice-language for violence is the psalm's key rhetorical strategy. These rulers have not abandoned the forms of justice — they still use scales (tefallesun) — but they have filled the scales with chamas ('violence') instead of mishpat ('justice'). The system looks functional from the outside; the contents have been switched.

verses.text_hebrew: זֹ֣רוּ רְשָׁעִ֣ים מֵרָ֑חֶם תָּע֥וּ מִ֝בֶּ֗טֶן דֹּבְרֵ֥י כָזָֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: The wicked are estranged from the womb: they go astray as soon as they be born, speaking lies.
verses.rendering:
The wicked go astray from the womb;
they wander off from birth, speaking lies.
translator_notes: The language parallels Psalm 51:7 (sinful from conception) but with a different purpose. David in Psalm 51 confessed his own condition in humility; here the psalmist describes the wicked's condition in accusation. me-rechem ('from the womb') and mi-beten ('from the belly/birth') indicate that their crookedness is not a late development but a lifelong orientation. dovrei khazav ('speakers of lies') — their defining characteristic from the start is falsehood.

verses.text_hebrew: חֲמַת־לָ֗מוֹ כִּדְמ֥וּת חֲמַת־נָחָ֑שׁ כְּמוֹ־פֶ֥תֶן חֵ֝רֵ֗שׁ יַאְטֵ֥ם אׇזְנֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Their poison is like the poison of a serpent: they are like the deaf adder that stoppeth her ear;
verses.rendering:
Their venom is like the venom of a serpent,
like a deaf cobra that stops its ear,
verses.expanded_rendering: The metaphor shifts from birth to biology. chamat lamo ('their venom/heat' — chamat can mean both 'venom' and 'rage') is compared to chamat nachash ('the venom of a serpent'). Then the image specifies: kemo feten cheresh ('like a deaf cobra'). The peten is a venomous snake, likely the Egyptian cobra. But this cobra is cheresh ('deaf') — it ya'tem ozno ('stops its ear'). The image draws on the practice of snake-charming: a skilled charmer could neutralize a snake through sound. But this snake has made itself deaf. It refuses to be charmed, refuses to respond to any voice, no matter how skilled. The corrupt rulers have made themselves immune to the voice of justice, wisdom, and appeal.
translator_notes: The deaf-cobra image is not about literal snake biology (snakes lack external ears and 'hear' through ground vibrations). It draws on the cultural practice of snake-charming, well attested in Egypt and Mesopotamia. The point is behavioral: this snake has chosen not to respond. The rulers have chosen not to hear.

verses.text_hebrew: אֲשֶׁ֣ר לֹא־יִ֭שְׁמַע לְק֣וֹל מְלַחֲשִׁ֑ים חוֹבֵ֥ר חֲ֝בָרִ֗ים מְחֻכָּֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Which will not hearken to the voice of charmers, charming never so wisely.
verses.rendering:
that will not hear the voice of charmers,
no matter how skillful the spellbinder.
verses.expanded_rendering: The cobra's deafness is absolute: lo yishma le-qol melachashim ('it will not hear the voice of whisperers/charmers'). melachashim are those who whisper incantations — the snake-charmers whose skill was renowned in the ancient world. Even a chover chavarim mechukkam ('a skillful binder of spells') — the most expert practitioner available — cannot reach this snake. The application is clear: no human counsel, no wise appeal, no prophetic voice will penetrate the self-deafened ears of these rulers. They have made themselves unreachable by any human means. Only God can deal with them now.
translator_notes: chover chavarim ('binder of spells') uses vocabulary from the realm of incantation and magical practice. The psalm does not endorse magic — it uses the image to make a point about the rulers' impenetrability. Even techniques designed to control the most dangerous creatures fail against willful moral deafness.

verses.text_hebrew: אֱלֹהִ֗ים הֲרׇס־שִׁנֵּ֥ימוֹ בְּפִ֑ימוֹ מַלְתְּע֥וֹת כְּ֝פִירִ֗ים נְתֹ֣ץ ׀ יְהֹוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Break their teeth, O God, in their mouth: break out the great teeth of the young lions, O LORD.
verses.rendering:
God, smash their teeth in their mouths!
Tear out the fangs of the young lions, LORD!
verses.expanded_rendering: The imprecation begins. The first image: haros shinneimo be-fimo ('smash their teeth in their mouths'). Teeth are the instruments of devouring — the rulers consume the vulnerable (cf. Psalm 53:5, 'they eat my people as they eat bread'). To break their teeth is to disarm them, to remove their capacity for predation. The parallel intensifies: malte'ot kefirim netos ('tear out the fangs of young lions'). malte'ot are the great jaw-teeth, the molars or fangs used for crushing. kefirim ('young lions') are lions in their prime — at maximum predatory power. God is asked to defang the predators at the peak of their strength.
translator_notes: The teeth-breaking image appears also in Psalm 3:7 ('you strike all my enemies on the jaw; you break the teeth of the wicked'). Teeth represent power — the power to consume, to destroy, to take. Breaking teeth is removing power from those who abuse it.

verses.text_hebrew: יִמָּאֲס֣וּ כְמוֹ־מַ֣יִם יִתְהַלְּכוּ־לָ֑מוֹ יִדְרֹ֥ךְ חִ֝צָּ֗ו כְּמ֣וֹ יִתְמֹלָֽלוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Let them melt away as waters which run continually: when he bendeth his bow to shoot his arrows, let them be as cut in pieces.
verses.rendering:
Let them vanish like water that flows away;
when they draw the bow, let their arrows be blunted.
translator_notes: The first of six rapid destruction-images. Water that flows away — poured on desert ground, it simply disappears. The wicked should become as irrecoverable as spilled water. Blunted arrows (yitmolalu, 'cut off, blunted') — even when they take hostile action (drawing the bow), their weapons fail.

verses.text_hebrew: כְּמ֣וֹ שַׁבְּל֣וּל תֶּ֣מֶס יַהֲלֹ֑ךְ נֵ֥פֶל אֵ֝֗שֶׁת בַּל־חָ֥זוּ שָֽׁמֶשׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: As a snail which melteth, let every one of them pass away: like the untimely birth of a woman, that they may not see the sun.
verses.rendering:
Like a snail that dissolves as it goes,
like a woman's stillborn child that never sees the sun.
translator_notes: Two more images: the shablul ('snail, slug') that temes yahalokh ('melts as it moves') — the ancient observation that a snail's trail looks like it is dissolving as it travels, leaving less of itself with every step. And the nefel eshet ('miscarriage, stillborn of a woman') that never sees the sun — a life that ends before it begins, that never enters the world of light and action. The stillborn image is harsh but not gratuitous: it describes a threat that never materializes, a potential for harm that is terminated before it can be realized.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּטֶ֤רֶם יָבִ֣ינוּ סִּֽירֹתֵיכֶ֣ם אָטָ֑ד כְּמוֹ־חַ֥י כְּמוֹ־חָ֝ר֗וֹן יִשְׂעָרֶֽנּוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Before your pots can feel the thorns, he shall take them away as with a whirlwind, both living, and in his wrath.
verses.rendering:
Before your pots feel the heat of thorns,
whether green or ablaze — he will sweep them away!
verses.expanded_rendering: The final destruction-image is the most visceral and sudden. In the desert, thornbushes (atad) were used as quick fuel under cooking pots (sirot). They burn hot and fast but briefly. The image: before the pots even register the heat of the thorns — before the fire accomplishes anything — God sweeps everything away in a storm-wind (yis'arennu). Whether the thorns are still green (chai, 'living') or already ablaze (charon, 'burning, wrathful'), it makes no difference. God acts before the wicked's plans reach fruition. The speed of divine intervention outpaces the speed of evil's execution.
translator_notes: This verse is among the most textually difficult in the Psalter. The images pile up faster than grammar can organize them. The essential point survives the difficulty: God's intervention is faster than evil's timetable. The storm arrives before the fire finishes its work.

verses.text_hebrew: יִשְׂמַ֣ח צַ֭דִּיק כִּי־חָזָ֣ה נָקָ֑ם פְּעָמָ֥יו יִ֝רְחַ֗ץ בְּדַ֣ם הָרָשָֽׁע׃
verses.text_kjv: The righteous shall rejoice when he seeth the vengeance: he shall wash his feet in the blood of the wicked.
verses.rendering:
The righteous will rejoice when they see vindication;
they will wash their feet in the blood of the wicked.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm's most difficult verse. The tsaddiq ('righteous one') yismach ('rejoices') when he sees naqam ('vindication, vengeance'). Then the image: pe'amav yirchats be-dam ha-rasha ('he will wash his feet in the blood of the wicked'). This is not a call to personal violence — it is the language of battlefield aftermath, where the victorious side walks through the field of the defeated. The image says: God's justice will be so thorough that the evidence of it will be everywhere. The righteous will walk through a world where the wicked's power has been completely broken. The 'blood' is metaphorical for the total overthrow of oppressive systems. The joy is not sadistic — it is the relief of people who have lived under crushing injustice and finally see it ended.
translator_notes: naqam ('vindication, vengeance') in Hebrew is not personal revenge but the restoration of violated justice. When God exercises naqam, he is setting right what was wrong. The emotion of the righteous is not bloodlust but the deep satisfaction of seeing justice finally done after prolonged injustice.

verses.text_hebrew: וְיֹאמַ֣ר אָ֭דָם אַךְ־פְּרִ֣י לַצַּדִּ֑יק אַ֥ךְ יֵשׁ־אֱ֝לֹהִ֗ים שֹׁפְטִ֥ים בָּאָֽרֶץ׃
verses.text_kjv: So that a man shall say, Verily there is a reward for the righteous: verily he is a God that judgeth in the earth.
verses.rendering:
And people will say,
"Yes — there is fruit for the righteous!
Yes — there is a God who judges on earth!"
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm closes with the vindication not just of the righteous but of the entire moral order. adam ('humanity, people in general') will speak — this is not a private realization but a public declaration. Two akh ('surely, indeed, yes') statements affirm what the psalm's enemies denied: there is peri la-tsaddiq ('fruit for the righteous') — righteousness is not futile; it yields results. And there is Elohim shofetim ba-arets ('a God who judges on earth') — the practical atheism of the wicked (Psalm 53:2, 'there is no God') is refuted by visible divine action. The final word is ba-arets ('on earth, in the land') — God's justice is not postponed to an afterlife but exercised here, in the world where the corruption happened.
translator_notes: The plural shofetim ('judging,' plural participle) with the singular Elohim is a known Hebrew construction where a plural form is used with God to express the fullness or intensity of the action. God judges with the combined force of all judges.
The psalm began by questioning whether rulers speak righteousness (v. 2); it ends by affirming that God judges righteously (v. 12). The human failure is answered by the divine success.

key_terms.note: The psalm's arc moves from the absence of human tsedeq (v. 2, rulers who do not speak righteousness) to the confirmation of divine justice (v. 12, God who judges on earth). tsedaqah is both the quality the rulers lacked and the outcome God guarantees.
